result: I keep thinking about the hour just before the world wakes up, when the sky is still a bruised violet and the only sound is the neighbor’s cat padding across the hood of my car. It’s the one pocket of time that hasn’t been monetized, repackaged, or pushed into a notification. No one sells me anything at 5:07 a.m.; no algorithm can guess what I want because, truthfully, I don’t want anything except the next breath of almost-cold air. In that hush, possibility feels like a physical organ, a second heart pumping maybe instead of blood. I imagine the day hasn’t decided what it will be yet—kind, cruel, tedious, ecstatic—and so neither have I. By 6:30 the leash tightens: emails breed, headlines scream, the coffee maker’s gurgle sounds like a timer starting. But before then, I’m undefeated. Sometimes I stand barefoot in the driveway just to feel the grit of yesterday’s asphalt, tiny eroded galaxies underfoot, and I think: every crisis, every love affair, every invoice began with someone standing in a quiet they didn’t recognize as precious. I try to store the sensation the way squirrels cache acorns, knowing I’ll need it when wintering through meetings, through headlines, through my own interior siren songs of inadequacy. We are told to be consistent, to build a brand, to optimize, but dawn reminds me that metamorphosis is the oldest subscription service: light arrives without login credentials and still redesigns the entire sky. I sign up again each morning, invisible ink on an invisible contract, renewable only by noticing.
